skill.name: render-info
skill.description: Explains how to use Blazor RendererInfo to detect static, server, WebAssembly, and WebView rendering contexts.
skill.summary: Guides Blazor developers on RendererInfo interactivity checks and render-mode-aware UI behavior.
skill.author: AndreHogberg

security_audit.summary: Static findings for Ruby backticks, weak cryptography, and system reconnaissance are false positives. SKILL.md is a short Blazor documentation file with Markdown inline code and Razor examples, with no executable scripts, network access, filesystem access, secrets handling, prompt injection, or malicious intent found.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Backticks Flagged as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The flagged locations are Markdown inline code or fenced Razor and C# examples. They describe Blazor RendererInfo usage and do not invoke a shell or execute external commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file contains only prose and sample Razor markup. No Ruby code, shell syntax, subprocess calls, or command execution APIs appear in the reviewed content.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Pattern
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The flagged lines discuss Blazor rendering concepts and do not reference hashing, encryption, random number generation, or cryptographic algorithms.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines contain documentation text about RendererInfo and render modes. No weak cryptographic API or algorithm is present.

low_findings.title: False Positive: System Reconnaissance Pattern
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The flagged WebView text names a Blazor Hybrid render environment. It does not collect host, user, process, network, or operating system details.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line lists a documented Blazor renderer name. There is no code path that queries system information or transmits collected data.

content.value_statement: Blazor components can behave incorrectly when static rendering and interactive rendering are mixed. This skill shows how RendererInfo helps adapt UI behavior across render modes.
actual_capabilities: Explains the purpose of Blazor RendererInfo in components.
Shows how to check whether a component is interactive.
Describes common RendererInfo.Name values for Blazor render modes.
Provides examples for disabling inputs during prerendering.
Compares static SSR content with interactive component rendering.
Notes the relationship between RendererInfo and AssignedRenderMode.
limitations: Only covers RendererInfo basics and common scenarios.
Does not provide a complete Blazor render mode architecture guide.
Does not include automated tests or runnable project files.
Requires .NET 8 or later for the documented API.

use_cases.title: Handle prerendered UI states
use_cases.description: Use RendererInfo.IsInteractive to disable controls or show waiting states until interactivity is available.

use_cases.title: Adapt components by render mode
use_cases.description: Choose simpler static HTML or richer interactive components based on the current renderer name.
use_cases.target_user: Frontend engineer working with Blazor SSR

use_cases.title: Document render behavior
use_cases.description: Explain how components behave across Static, Server, WebAssembly, and WebView contexts.

prompt_templates.title: Explain RendererInfo basics
prompt_templates.prompt: Explain how Blazor RendererInfo helps detect whether my component is currently interactive.

prompt_templates.title: Fix prerendered buttons
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my Blazor component and suggest where RendererInfo.IsInteractive should disable buttons during prerendering.

prompt_templates.title: Design render-mode-aware content
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me design a Blazor component that renders a static form during SSR and an interactive component after activation.

prompt_templates.title: Audit render mode assumptions
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze this Blazor component for incorrect assumptions about Static, Server, WebAssembly, and WebView render modes.

output_examples.input: I need to prevent users from clicking a submit button before Blazor becomes interactive.
output_examples.output: Use RendererInfo.IsInteractive to bind the disabled state. Show a clear waiting state during prerendering, then enable the button after interactivity starts.

output_examples.input: I need a search component that works with static SSR and interactive rendering.
output_examples.output: Render a plain HTML form when the renderer is Static. Render the richer search component when the renderer is interactive.

output_examples.input: I want to explain RendererInfo.Name to my team.
output_examples.output: Describe it as the current renderer label. Common values include Static, Server, WebAssembly, and WebView.

best_practices: Check RendererInfo.IsInteractive before enabling event-driven UI.
Provide useful static HTML fallback content for prerendered pages.
Keep render-mode checks small and close to the UI behavior they affect.
anti_patterns: Assuming event handlers work during static server-side rendering.
Using renderer names as a replacement for feature detection in unrelated logic.
Hiding all content during prerendering instead of providing a useful fallback.

faq.question: What does this skill cover?
faq.answer: It covers basic Blazor RendererInfo usage for interactivity checks and renderer name detection.

faq.question: Which .NET version is required?
faq.answer: The documented RendererInfo API is available in .NET 8 and later.

faq.question: Does it include runnable project files?
faq.answer: No. It provides documentation and short Razor examples only.

faq.question: Can it help with Blazor prerendering issues?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains how to disable or change UI until the component becomes interactive.

faq.question: Does it support Blazor Hybrid?
faq.answer: It mentions WebView as a RendererInfo.Name value for Blazor Hybrid applications.

faq.question: Is this skill safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes. The audit found no executable code, network access, prompt injection, or confirmed dangerous behavior.
